risk_of_bias.support_text: Quote: "participants were consented in-hospital and randomly assigned, in double-blind fashion, to either a 10-day course ... of hydrocortisone or placebo." Comment: The study states that participants were randomly assigned, but it does not describe the sequence-generation method (e.g., computer-generated list, random numbers, blocks, or other explicit random method). Under Cochrane guidance, this is insufficient to confirm a truly random allocation sequence, so the risk remains unclear.

_evidence.method_quote: Following eligibility determination, participants were consented in-hospital and randomly assigned, in double-blind fashion, to either a 10-day course (plus a 6-day taper period) of hydrocortisone or placebo.
_evidence.method_summary: Participants were randomly assigned to hydrocortisone or placebo, but the method of sequence generation is not described.

risk_of_bias.support_text: Quote: "Following eligibility determination, participants were consented in-hospital and randomly assigned, in double-blind fashion, to either a 10-day course (plus a 6-day taper period) of hydrocortisone or placebo." Comment: The article states random assignment and double-blind treatment, but it does not describe any allocation concealment mechanism (e.g., central allocation, sequentially numbered opaque sealed envelopes, or identical containers) that would prevent recruiters from foreseeing the next assignment. By Cochrane criteria, absence of concealment detail is insufficient to judge Low or High, so this remains Unclear risk.

_evidence.note: The article states participants were “randomly assigned, in double-blind fashion,” but gives no description of how the allocation sequence was concealed from recruiters.

risk_of_bias.domain: Blinding of participants and personnel (performance bias)
risk_of_bias.support_text: Quote: "randomly assigned, in double-blind fashion, to either a 10-day course (plus a 6-day taper period) of hydrocortisone or placebo" and "Following consent, the nurse administered the first oral dose [20 mg hydrocortisone ... or placebo capsules]." Comment: The study explicitly states that allocation was double-blind and used matching placebo capsules, indicating participants and personnel delivering the intervention were intended to be blinded. The reported outcomes included several subjective patient-reported measures, but because the trial describes successful double-blinding rather than absent/incomplete blinding, this supports a low risk judgement for performance bias.

_evidence.blinding_quote:
Following eligibility determination, participants were consented in-hospital and randomly assigned, in double-blind fashion, to either a 10-day course (plus a 6-day taper period) of hydrocortisone or placebo.

Following consent, the nurse administered the first oral dose [20 mg hydrocortisone (Cortef, Pharmacia) or placebo capsules] within 12 h of hospital admission.
outcome_categories: health-related quality of life (SF-36 general health)
subjective_outcomes: health-related quality of life (SF-36 general health)

risk_of_bias.support_text: Quote: "Participants were administered the Clinician-Administered PTSD Scale (CAPS) to assess incidence of PTSD and PTSD symptoms... Participants were left a packet of self-report measures... Self-report measures included the Center for Epidemiological Studies—Depression Scale (CES-D) to assess depressive symptoms and the SF-36 to assess health-related quality of life." Comment: The study reports a mix of clinician-administered and self-report outcomes, including clearly subjective outcomes (PTSD symptoms, depression, and quality of life). Although the paper says the trial was "double-blind," it does not explicitly state that the outcome assessor for these follow-up assessments was blinded, and the self-reported outcomes were completed by participants themselves. Under the worst-outcome rule, the presence of unblinded/self-reported subjective outcomes makes detection bias High risk.

_evidence.assessor_blinding_quote: The 1-month and 3-month follow-up assessments were identical and took place in participants’ homes. Participants were administered the Clinician-Administered PTSD Scale (CAPS)51 to assess incidence of PTSD and PTSD symptoms, and were asked whether they had ever previously received help from a mental health professional. Participants were left a packet of self-report measures and a stamped envelope in which to return completed forms. Self-report measures included the Center for Epidemiological Studies—Depression Scale52 to assess depressive symptoms and the SF-36 to assess health-related quality of life.53

outcomes.label: health-related quality of life (SF-36 general health)

risk_of_bias.support_text: Quote: "A series of repeated measures, or analyses of covariance (ANCOVAs), were conducted on participants who completed the entire study protocol (completer analyses)" and "At 1 month posttrauma, 6 participants had dropped from the placebo group and 7 had dropped from the hydrocortisone group, while at 3 months a total of 9 had dropped from the placebo group and 12 had dropped from the hydrocortisone group." Comment: The primary analyses were completer-only, and the article reports substantial post-randomization attrition on the analysed outcomes: 13/64 not retained by 1 month (about 20.3%) and 21/64 by 3 months (about 32.8%). This crosses the high-risk threshold for total loss at the analysed time point, and there is no evidence of an appropriate missing-data approach (e.g. imputation or mixed models) to protect against bias.

per_arm_flow.reasons: At 1 month posttrauma, 7 had dropped from the hydrocortisone group; one complained of dizziness and asked to discontinue participation (from the hydrocortisone group), and the remaining drop-outs could not be contacted after numerous attempts. At 3 months, 12 had dropped from the hydrocortisone group; drop-outs between the 1- and 3-month time points were all due to inability to reach participants after repeated attempts.

At 1 month posttrauma, 6 participants had dropped from the placebo group; the remaining drop-outs could not be contacted after numerous attempts. At 3 months, 9 had dropped from the placebo group; drop-outs between the 1- and 3-month time points were all due to inability to reach participants after repeated attempts.

_evidence.notes: Primary analyses were described as 'completer analyses' on participants who completed the entire study protocol. The article reports attrition at both 1-month and 3-month follow-up; total_loss_pct computed from 13/64 not retained at 1 month (the first analysed follow-up).
